title: About data.js
text:
data.js is a small JavaScript library for loading, transforming and combining tabular and geospatial data directly in the browser — CSV, JSON, GeoJSON, Parquet, GeoPackage and more, with no build step and no backend required.



data.js was created by Guenter Richter as the data-processing engine behind iXMaps, the declarative thematic-mapping framework. It grew from handling plain CSV/JSON into a broader set of loaders as iXMaps itself took on more geospatial formats — GeoParquet, GeoPackage, FlatGeobuf — culminating most recently in DuckDB WASM-backed bounding-box queries against remote GeoParquet files, so large datasets can be explored without downloading them in full (see Remote Parquet by Bounding Box).
While it was built for iXMaps, data.js has no dependency on it and works entirely standalone.





All loading and processing happens client-side:

No backend or server round-trip required
Easy to deploy — a single <script> tag
Data stays in the user’s browser




One consistent API (Data.feed / Data.Table) regardless of whether the source is a CSV file, a GeoJSON object, or a multi-hundred-megabyte remote Parquet file.



data.js covers the filtering, pivoting and joining operations that come up constantly in real data work — it doesn’t try to be a general SQL engine or a full GIS toolkit.





JavaScript — no required build step
Papa Parse — CSV parsing
DuckDB WASM — Parquet, GeoParquet and GeoPackage reading, including the httpfs extension for genuine HTTP range reads
flatgeobuf / geobuf — binary geospatial format reading
proj4js — on-demand CRS reprojection
jQuery — optional, used by some legacy loading paths




data.js ships as part of the iXMaps project and is released under the BSD 3-Clause License — free for personal and commercial use, provided the original copyright notice is included; the original authors’ names may not be used to promote derived products.





Guenter Richter — library design and maintenance




Papa Parse by Matt Holt
DuckDB WASM by the DuckDB team
proj4js
jQuery





Bug reports, feature requests and documentation improvements are welcome — visit the GitHub repository to get involved.




iXMaps Documentation — the mapping framework data.js was built for
JSDoc API reference — generated from the source, method-by-method





GitHub Issues: github.com/gjrichter/data.js/issues


data.js — loading and transforming data, right in the browser.

data.js — loading and transforming data, right in the browser.

title: Examples
text: Working code for the most common data.js use cases. Each example is self-contained — copy it into a <script> tag after including data.js from the CDN (see Quick Start).

title: Examples

Examples
section: Load a remote GeoParquet file by bounding box

title: Examples
Examples
API Reference
text: Complete reference for the data.js JavaScript API. For narrative walkthroughs see Loading Data, Transforming Tables and Combining Sources.

title: API Reference
text:
Loaders

Data.feed(options)
Loads data from a URL. Returns a Data.Feed instance — chain .load()/.error().
Data.feed({ source: url, type: "csv" })
    .load(function (mydata) { /* Data.Table */ })
    .error(function (e) { /* handle failure */ });
Options:



Option
Type
Description




source (or src, url, ext)
string
The data URL


type
string
Source format — see Source Types


cache
boolean
Default true


parser
object
CSV only: Papa Parse options, passed straight through


bbox, columns, crs, proj4, maxRows
—
Parquet bbox mode only — see Remote Parquet by Bounding Box





Data.object(options)
Same shape as Data.feed, but source is a JavaScript value already in memory rather than a URL. Chain .import()/.error().
Data.object({ source: response, type: "json" }).import(function (mydata) { ... });
For binary types (parquet, geoparquet, gpkg, flatgeobuf, geobuf), source must already be an ArrayBuffer.


Data.import(options)
Synchronous shortcut, equivalent to new Data.Object(options).import().feed.dbtable. Returns a Data.Table immediately — no callback.
var table = Data.import({ source: response, type: "json" });
Only reliable for synchronous parsers (json, csv, geojson, and similar) — see the callout in Loading Data.

title: API Reference
text:
Data.Feed (the object returned by Data.feed())








Method
Signature
Purpose




load(callback)
(function(Data.Table))
Starts the load; resolves with a Data.Table


error(callback)
(function(error))
Sets the failure handler


getFileSize(callback?)
(function(bytes)?)
HEAD/Range request for Content-Length without downloading the body. Returns a Promise if no callback is given


broker(options)
(object)
Returns a provider (Data.Broker instance) bound to this feed as parent



Once .load() has resolved, these table-passthrough convenience methods are also available directly on the feed object (they operate on the loaded Data.Table): .column(), .select(), .aggregate(), .pivot(), .subtable(), .revert(), .reverse(), .addTimeColumns().

title: API Reference
text:
Data.Table (returned by a successful load)



Method
Signature
Purpose




select(query)
(string)
SQL-like WHERE filter — returns a new table. See syntax


pivot(options)
({lead, keep, sum, cols, value, calc, forced})
Build a pivot table


aggregate(valueCol, groupCols)
(string, "col1\|col2") or ({column/value, lead, calc})
Sum/mean a value column grouped by one or more columns


condense(leadCol, options)
(string, {keep, calc})
Collapse duplicate rows by a lead column


sort(column, flag)
(string, "DOWN"?)
Sort rows; omit flag for ascending


filter(callback)
(function(row)->bool)
Row-level filter via callback


addColumn(options, callback)
({source, destination}, function)
Derive a new column


addRow(options)
({colName: value, ...})
Append a row


subtable(options)
({fields:[...]}) or ({columns:[idx,...]})
New table with only the given columns


groupColumns(options)
({source:[...], destination})
Sum several numeric columns into a new one


append(sourceTable)
(Data.Table)
Append another table’s rows (identical field names/order required)


column(name)
(string)
Returns a Data.Column handle


columnNames()
()
Array of column name strings


columnIndex(name)
(string)
Numeric index of a column


lookup(value, option)
(value, {value, lookup})
Single-value lookup


lookupArray(valueCol, lookupCol)
(string, string) or ({key,value,calc})
Object mapping lookup values → value-column values


lookupStringArray(valueCol, lookupCol)
(string, string) or ({key,value})
Like lookupArray, concatenating duplicates into a comma-separated string


toKeyValue(option)
({key, value})
Thin wrapper over lookupArray


getArray()
()
Table as a 2D array (row 0 = column names)


setArray(dataA)
(2D array)
Populate the table from a 2D array; returns this


json()
()
Table as an array of {colName: value, ...} row objects


revert() / reverse()
()
Reverse row order (identical implementations)


addTimeColumns(options)
({source, create:[...]})
Derive date/year/month/day/hour columns from a timestamp source column

title: API Reference
text:
Data.Column (returned by table.column(name))








Method
Signature
Purpose




values()
()
Array of every value in the column


uniqueValues()
()
Array of unique values in the column


map(callback)
(function(value, row, index))
In-place transform of every cell


rename(name)
(string)
Rename the column


remove()
()
Delete the column from the table

title: API Reference
text:
Data.provider() — parallel multi-source loading








Method
Signature
Purpose




addSource(url, type)
(string, string)
Register one source to load; chainable


load(callback)
(function(Data.Table[]))
Load all sources, then call back with an array of tables in addSource order


realize(callback)
(function(Data.Table[]))
Older name for load(callback) — identical behavior


error(callback)
(function(url))
Shared error handler for any source’s load failure


notify(callback)
(function(query))
Progress callback, invoked as each source starts/completes



Factory: Data.provider() — preferred. Data.broker() is an older name for the same factory; both return a Data.Broker instance. See Combining Sources for a worked example.

title: API Reference
text:
Data.Merger — joining already-loaded tables








Method
Signature
Purpose




addSource(table, option)
(Data.Table, {lookup, columns, label})
Register a loaded table as a merge source


setOutputColumns(columns)
(array)
Restrict/order the final table’s columns


realize(callback)
(function(Data.Table))
Perform the join; returns the merged table


error(callback)
(function(exception))
Error handler



Factory: Data.merger(). See Combining Sources for a worked example.

title: API Reference
text:
Utility members







Member
Purpose




Data.version
Library version string


Data.log(message)
Internal progress logging


Data.errors
Array collecting errors from feeds without an explicit .error() handler


Data.cleanupDuckDB()
Manually tears down the shared DuckDB WASM instance (console utility)


Data.noConflict()
Restores whatever window.Data held before this library loaded

title: Transforming Tables
text: Every successful load resolves to a Data.Table. This guide covers the methods you’ll reach for most to filter, reshape and summarize it. For the full method signature list see the API Reference.

title: Transforming Tables
Transforming Tables
Transforming Tables
Transforming Tables
Transforming Tables
Transforming Tables
text:
Sorting: sort(column, flag)
var newestFirst = mydata.sort("date", "DOWN");   // omit the flag for ascending

title: Transforming Tables
Transforming Tables
section: Deriving a new column: addColumn(options, callback)

title: Transforming Tables
text:
Picking columns: subtable(options)
var slim = mydata.subtable({ fields: ["id", "subtype", "height"] });

title: Transforming Tables
text:
Other useful methods







Method
Purpose




columnNames()
Array of all column names


columnIndex(name)
Numeric index of a column


lookup(value, {value, lookup})
Single-value lookup against a value/lookup column pair


lookupArray(valueCol, lookupCol)
Build an object mapping lookup values to value-column values


groupColumns({source, destination})
Sum several existing numeric columns into a new one


append(otherTable)
Append another table’s rows (must have identical field names/order)


json()
Return the table as an array of {colName: value, ...} row objects


getArray() / setArray(array)
Read/write the table as a plain 2D array (row 0 = column names)









Noterevert() and reverse() are the same thing



Both reverse row order — they’re currently identical implementations, not two different behaviors. Either name works.



Next: Combining Sources to load or join more than one table at once.

title: Loading Data
text: data.js has three loading entry points. They all end up producing the same thing — a Data.Table — but differ in where the source data comes from and whether loading is asynchronous.

title: Loading Data
Loading Data
section: Data.object(options) — load from data you already have

title: Loading Data
Loading Data
text:
Supported source types
Pass one of these as type to any of the three loaders above. See Source Types for the full options reference (the parquet row in particular has several bbox-mode-only options covered in Remote Parquet by Bounding Box).



type
What it loads




"csv"
Plain-text CSV (delimiters , and ;), parsed with Papa Parse


"json"
A JSON object


"geojson"
A GeoJSON object


"topojson"
TopoJSON


"kml"
Keyhole Markup Language


"gml"
Geography Markup Language


"jsonl" / "ndjson"
Newline-delimited JSON


"jsonstat"
JSON-stat formatted object


"rss"
XML RSS feed


"parquet"
Parquet file, via DuckDB WASM. With bbox set, queries a remote URL directly instead of downloading it — see Remote Parquet by Bounding Box


"geoparquet"
GeoParquet file, via DuckDB WASM, converted to GeoJSON


"gpkg"
GeoPackage file, via DuckDB WASM’s spatial extension


"flatgeobuf" / "fgb"
FlatGeobuf binary geospatial format


"geobuf" / "pbf"
Geobuf (Protocol Buffer) geospatial format


"jsonDB"
data.js’s own internal table format (used internally, e.g. when passing an already-built Data.Table)



Type names are case-insensitive variants of the above (CSV, GeoJSON, TOPOJSON, etc. all work).






NoteOmitting type



For a plain URL, data.js can auto-detect the type from the file extension for a few formats (.jsonl/.ndjson). For everything else, always pass type explicitly.



Next: Transforming Tables to filter, pivot and reshape whatever you just loaded.

title: Welcome to data.js Documentation
text:
data.js is a little JavaScript library to load and transform data. It reads open formats — CSV, JSON, GeoJSON, Parquet, GeoPackage and more — from a URL or an in-memory object, and provides a small set of table operations (select, pivot, aggregate, sort, joins) for exploring and reshaping the result, all in the browser with no build step.


At the end of this documentation, you will be able to:

Load tabular and geospatial data from a URL or from data you already have in memory
Query remote GeoParquet files by bounding box, without downloading the whole file
Filter, pivot, aggregate and sort tables with a small SQL-like syntax
Combine multiple sources — either loading several in parallel, or joining tables you already have
Recognize the handful of gotchas that trip people up (documented in Troubleshooting)




data.js consists of three main parts:


Get data into the library, from a URL (Data.feed) or from an already-in-memory object (Data.object), across a wide range of formats:

Tabular: CSV, JSON, JSON-stat, JSONL/NDJSON
Geospatial: GeoJSON, TopoJSON, KML, GML, Parquet/GeoParquet, GeoPackage, FlatGeobuf, Geobuf
Feeds: RSS

Need several sources loaded in parallel before you continue? Data.provider() loads several URLs at once and calls you back only once they’ve all resolved. See Combining Sources.






data.js does its own coordinate work only for the remote-parquet bbox feature (bounding boxes and geometry are reprojected automatically when the source uses a projected CRS, e.g. EPSG:3035). For every other format, input geometry is used as-is — reproject to EPSG:4326 yourself first if needed. See Remote Parquet by Bounding Box.






Every successful load resolves to a Data.Table: a simple {fields, records} structure with chainable methods for reshaping it — select() (SQL-like WHERE filtering), pivot(), aggregate(), condense(), sort(), addColumn(), and more. See Transforming Tables and the API Reference.



Two different ways to work with more than one source:

Data.provider() loads several URLs in parallel and calls you back once all of them resolve.
Data.Merger joins tables you’ve already loaded, by a shared lookup key.

See Combining Sources.




data.js is a good fit for:

Data journalists and researchers who need to filter, pivot and join open data without spinning up a backend
Map/visualization developers — data.js is the data-processing engine behind iXMaps, but works standalone too
Anyone loading Parquet/GeoParquet in the browser who wants only the rows in view, not the whole file




data.js is not:

A mapping or charting library — it has no rendering of its own (pair it with iXMaps, Leaflet, D3, or anything else)
A general-purpose SQL engine — select()/pivot() cover common filtering/reshaping needs, not arbitrary joins or subqueries
A build-time tool — everything runs client-side, at request time




data.js is built on:

JavaScript — no required build step; a single <script> tag is enough
Papa Parse — CSV parsing
DuckDB WASM — Parquet, GeoParquet and GeoPackage reading, including the httpfs extension for ranged remote reads
flatgeobuf / geobuf — binary geospatial format reading
proj4js — on-demand coordinate reprojection for remote-parquet bbox queries
jQuery — optional, used for some legacy loading paths




data.js has no separate license file of its own in the repository; it ships as part of the iXMaps project, released under the BSD 3-Clause License.









TipNew to data.js?



Start with the Quick Start Guide to load and query your first dataset in a few lines.








NoteLooking for API Reference?



Check the API Reference for the complete method reference, or Source Types for every supported type and its options.








ImportantNeed examples?



Browse Examples for copy-paste working code for the most common use cases.





Include data.js in any HTML page with a single <script> tag — no installation, no build step:
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/gjrichter/data.js/data.js"></script>




GitHub Repository: github.com/gjrichter/data.js
Issues and Questions: GitHub Issues
JSDoc reference: gjrichter.github.io/data.js/docs


Ready to start? Head over to the Quick Start Guide to load your first dataset!

title: Quick Start
text: This guide walks you through loading and querying your first dataset with data.js — from a blank HTML file to a filtered result you can read values from.

title: Quick Start
Quick Start
Quick Start
section: Step 3: Filter and read the result

title: Remote Parquet by Bounding Box
text: Large GeoParquet datasets (a country’s worth of buildings, for example) are often too big to download in full just to show whatever’s in the current map viewport. data.js’s parquet loader supports a bounding-box mode: pass a bbox, and instead of downloading the whole file, data.js queries it directly with DuckDB WASM, reading only the row groups and columns that actually intersect your box.

title: Remote Parquet by Bounding Box
Remote Parquet by Bounding Box
text:
How it works
Without bbox, type: "parquet" downloads the entire file before running any query — fine for small files, wasteful for large ones. With bbox set, data.js:

Ensures DuckDB WASM is loaded, with the httpfs extension installed. This is what makes genuine HTTP range reads possible — without it, DuckDB WASM’s default file handler pulls the whole remote file into memory before it can run any query at all, footer included.
Probes the file’s schema (a cheap LIMIT 0 query — footer only, no row data) to find a bbox helper column (a STRUCT field with xmin/ymin/xmax/ymax sub-fields — the GeoParquet covering.bbox convention) and the geometry column.
Reads the GeoParquet geo metadata to detect the source CRS, if any.
Runs a WHERE query against the bbox helper column, so DuckDB can skip pages that can’t possibly match, then returns only the requested columns plus geometry.







ImportantRequires a GeoParquet bbox helper column



If the file has no bbox helper column, data.js logs a warning and falls back to a full download rather than failing outright. Bbox-mode’s network savings only apply to files that follow the GeoParquet covering.bbox convention.

title: Remote Parquet by Bounding Box
text:
Options









Option
Type
Required
Description




bbox
[minX, minY, maxX, maxY]
for bbox mode
Always EPSG:4326, regardless of the source file’s CRS


columns
array of strings
no
Restrict the result to these columns (the geometry column is always included automatically). Omit to get every non-list column


crs
string, e.g. "EPSG:3035"
no
Override the auto-detected source CRS


proj4
proj4 definition string
no
Override with a raw proj4 definition — takes precedence over crs


maxRows
number
no
Abort with a clear error instead of running the query if the bbox would select more rows than this

title: Remote Parquet by Bounding Box
text:
CRS reprojection
Many large GeoParquet datasets use a projected CRS rather than plain lon/lat — EUBUCCO v0.2, for example, uses EPSG:3035 (ETRS89-extended / LAEA Europe). data.js handles this automatically:

Your bbox (always EPSG:4326) is transformed into the source CRS before the query — using all four corners of the box, not just two, since projections curve straight edges.
Returned geometries are transformed back to EPSG:4326.
The source CRS is auto-detected from the file’s GeoParquet metadata; built-in proj4js definitions are bundled for the CRSes most likely to show up in European open data: EPSG:3035, 3857 (Web Mercator), 2154 (Lambert-93, France), 25832/25833 (ETRS89 / UTM 32N, 33N), 32632/32633 (WGS84 / UTM 32N, 33N).
proj4js itself is only loaded (from a CDN) the first time a reprojection is actually needed — no extra cost for files already in EPSG:4326.

If your file uses a CRS not in that built-in list, pass crs (an EPSG code) or proj4 (a full proj4 definition string) explicitly:
Data.feed({
    source: someUrl,
    type:   "parquet",
    bbox:   [minX, minY, maxX, maxY],
    crs:    "EPSG:25831"   // not in the built-in list — data.js will still need a definition
}).load(...);






Without a recognized crs/proj4, data.js reports a clear error rather than silently returning wrong results — it never guesses at a projection.

title: Remote Parquet by Bounding Box
Remote Parquet by Bounding Box
text:
File size limit
Remote files ≥ 2 GB are only usable with httpfs successfully loaded — without it, DuckDB WASM’s default file handler would need to buffer the whole file in memory, which fails outright above ~2 GB. In normal use httpfs loads automatically and this limit doesn’t apply; it only kicks in as a fallback safeguard if the extension fails to load for some reason (offline environment, blocked CDN, etc.).

Next: Transforming Tables to filter and reshape the result once it’s loaded.

title: Combining Sources
text: data.js has two different tools for working with more than one source, solving two different problems:

title: Combining Sources
section: Data.provider(): load several sources in parallel

title: Combining Sources
Source Types
text: Reference for the type option accepted by Data.feed(), Data.object() and Data.import(). See Loading Data for how the three loaders differ.

title: Source Types
text:
Tabular



type
Parser
Notes




"csv"
Papa Parse
Delimiters , and ; both supported. Pass parser (an object) to forward Papa Parse options


"json"
native
Any JSON value/object


"jsonl" / "ndjson"
native
Newline-delimited JSON


"jsonstat"
json-stat
Lazy-loads the json-stat parser script on first use


"rss"
native XML
RSS feed

title: Source Types
text:
Geospatial — text-based








type
Parser
Notes




"geojson"
native
A GeoJSON object


"topojson"
native
Converted internally to GeoJSON-equivalent structures


"kml"
native XML
Keyhole Markup Language


"gml"
native XML
Geography Markup Language

title: Source Types
text:
Geospatial — binary, via DuckDB WASM



type
Notes




"parquet"
Parquet file. Without bbox, downloads and parses the whole file. With bbox set, queries a remote URL directly via HTTP range reads instead — see Remote Parquet by Bounding Box for the full option set (bbox, columns, crs, proj4, maxRows)


"geoparquet"
GeoParquet file, converted to GeoJSON. Always a full download (bbox mode is parquet-only)


"gpkg"
GeoPackage file, read via DuckDB WASM’s spatial extension

title: Source Types
text:
Geospatial — binary, other parsers



type
Notes




"flatgeobuf" / "fgb"
FlatGeobuf binary format


"geobuf" / "pbf"
Geobuf (Protocol Buffer) binary format

title: Source Types
text:
Internal







type
Notes




"jsonDB"
data.js’s own {fields, records} table structure — used when passing an already-built Data.Table-shaped object back into the library









NoteCase-insensitive



Type names accept case variants — "CSV", "GeoJSON", "TOPOJSON" all work the same as their lowercase forms.



See the API Reference for the loader method signatures, or Examples for copy-paste code using several of these types.

title: Troubleshooting
text: Data.import() is synchronous and returns immediately — it never waits for anything async. It works for parsers that run synchronously (csv, json, geojson and similar), but for a genuinely asynchronous parser (e.g. jsonstat, which lazy-loads its own script) it returns before the data exists. Use Data.object({...}).import(callback) instead — see Loading Data.

title: Troubleshooting
text: Data.import() is synchronous and returns immediately — it never waits for anything async. It works for parsers that run synchronously (csv, json, geojson and similar), but for a genuinely asynchronous parser (e.g. jsonstat, which lazy-loads its own script) it returns before the data exists. Use Data.object({...}).import(callback) instead — see Loading Data.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Data.provider() doesn’t have .select()/.column()
A Data.provider() instance (a Data.Broker under the hood — Data.Broker is the older name for the same thing) does not expose the Data.Table/Data.Feed convenience methods directly, despite what an internal comment in the source suggests. Work with the individual Data.Table objects handed to your .load(callback) (or .realize(callback), the older name for the same thing) — see Combining Sources.

title: Troubleshooting
section: A remote parquet file loads slowly, or fails on a large file
text:
A remote parquet file loads slowly, or fails on a large file
Two different things can be going on:

No bbox was passed. Without it, type: "parquet" always downloads the entire file first. If you only need part of a large file, add a bbox — see Remote Parquet by Bounding Box.
The file is ≥ 2 GB and httpfs failed to load. This is a fallback-only limit; it shouldn’t come up in normal use, but if you see an error mentioning “2 GB”, the httpfs extension failed to install (offline environment, blocked CDN) and data.js refused to attempt a full in-memory download of a file that size.

title: Troubleshooting
section: A bbox query returns no rows, or falls back to a full download
text:
A bbox query returns no rows, or falls back to a full download
If the source file has no GeoParquet bbox helper column (the covering.bbox convention), data.js can’t prune anything and falls back to downloading the whole file — this is deliberate, not a bug. Check the file’s GeoParquet metadata, or use a source known to include bbox columns (e.g. EUBUCCO).
If rows come back empty even though you expect matches, double-check your bbox — it must always be [minX, minY, maxX, maxY] in EPSG:4326, never in the source file’s native CRS.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
“Unsupported source CRS” error
The source file’s CRS was detected (or you passed crs explicitly) but isn’t one of the built-in proj4 definitions. Pass proj4 with a full proj4 definition string instead of crs — see Remote Parquet by Bounding Box.

title: Troubleshooting
section: The three source-type JSDoc lists don’t quite agree
text:
The three source-type JSDoc lists don’t quite agree
Data.Object, Data.Import and Data.Feed’s inline documentation blocks are three separate copies that have drifted slightly out of sync with each other over time. The Source Types page in this documentation reflects the actual dispatch logic in the source, which is authoritative — trust it over any single inline comment.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
revert() vs reverse()
Both exist, and both currently do the same thing (reverse row order). This isn’t a bug you need to work around — either name is safe to use.
